jokes.text: Opening song lyric: 'I'm not coming to your party because your party sucks'
jokes.setup: Typical sitcom opening music plays
jokes.punchline: The lyric ironically foreshadows the entire episode's plot about party-hopping because parties suck
jokes.explanation: Clever metatextual joke that works on rewatch once you know the episode is about FOMO and leaving bad parties. Good economy, but low character integration since it's a song lyric not tied to Abbi or Ilana specifically.

jokes.text: Ilana FaceTimes Abbi to reveal a nose ring, Abbi's first reaction is 'that's very hot and 2004 of you'
jokes.setup: Ilana excitedly reveals she got a nose ring like it's the most amazing thing ever
jokes.punchline: Abbi's flat, hilariously specific 'hot and 2004' — validating but also completely dunking on the trend
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Abbi's dry wit and the specific cultural cringe of early-2000s nostalgia. 'Hot and 2004' is a highly quotable, economical dismissal that works because it's simultaneously a compliment and a roast. Character integration is strong — only Abbi would phrase it exactly this way, with that mixture of affection and exasperation.

jokes.text: Abbi reveals she 'stayed in and organized her life' by reinforcing boob buttons on button-downs and wiping down every surface of her apartment
jokes.setup: Ilana expresses FOMO guilt; Abbi defends staying in by saying she got a lot done
jokes.punchline: The specific mundane tasks she lists — 'boob buttons' and wiping down surfaces — are both pathetically domestic and oddly specific, making 'staying in and organizing life' sound even sadder than doing nothing
jokes.explanation: 'Boob buttons' is a hilariously specific and very Abbi detail — it's a real practical problem that would only occur to someone extremely pragmatic and slightly neurotic about her appearance. It makes Abbi's sad Friday night feel both painfully relatable and uniquely her. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Abbi also 'combined all the weed remnants to make one giant super blunt'
jokes.setup: Abbi is listing her productive stay-in activities: boob buttons, wiping surfaces...
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from mundanely domestic to drug-related resourcefulness as a perfectly matter-of-fact third item
jokes.explanation: The escalation from domestic tasks to weed consolidation is the classic Rule of Three payoff — two normal items then the absurd third. The deadpan delivery implied by the transcript makes it land. Not quite quotable on its own but earns a solid laugh in context.

jokes.text: Ilana's parting shot: 'Ugh, yeah, try not to pierce your pussy while I'm gone' followed by Abbi's 'Ew, what? Did you just leave?'
jokes.setup: Ilana says something outrageously raunchy as a casual goodbye
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'Did you just leave?' — implying Ilana dropped the line and immediately hung up, refusing to deal with Abbi's reaction. The joke is as much about Ilana's hit-and-run communication style as the vulgarity.
jokes.explanation: The real joke isn't the shock value of 'pierce your pussy' — it's Ilana deploying maximum vulgarity and instantly vanishing. Abbi's bewildered 'Did you just leave?' perfectly captures their dynamic. Economy is excellent; the punchline is in the reaction, not the crude line itself.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I cannot wait to smoke that one day after work.' / Ilana: 'Work? Jesus fu-- I knew I was forgetting something.'
jokes.setup: Abbi mentions work, assuming Ilana is also employed and responsible
jokes.punchline: Ilana has entirely forgotten she has a job today — the word 'work' is a foreign object crashing into her consciousness
jokes.explanation: The structure is clean: Abbi's casual mention triggers Ilana's total existential surprise. The truncated 'Jesus fu--' lands as a genuine panic response. Very efficient comedy, character-perfect.

jokes.text: Trey says 'You're fired' to Abbi on her first day training a new cleaner, then immediately says 'I'm kidding, bazinga'
jokes.setup: Trey interrupts Abbi mid-orientation with what sounds like a termination
jokes.punchline: 'Bazinga' — a Big Bang Theory reference used by a bro gym manager who clearly thinks he's hilarious. The joke is that Trey thinks this is cutting-edge comedy.
jokes.explanation: Trey saying 'bazinga' is a character joke — it reveals exactly the kind of guy he is. Abbi's confused 'I don't know what that means' doubles down on it. The layered irony is that Broad City is itself a Comedy Central show mocking Big Bang Theory's style of nerd comedy. Solid mid-tier joke.

jokes.text: Trey describes the cleaning emergency: 'Somebody barfed on an exercise ball and then it bounced down two flights of stairs'
jokes.setup: Trey says there's a 'big ol' cleaning disaster'
jokes.punchline: The specific physics of barf on an exercise ball bouncing down two flights of stairs — it's absurdly visual and funny because it's also clearly a real thing that could happen at a gym
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific visual gag. The trajectory detail — that the barf-covered ball *bounced down two flights of stairs* — is what elevates this from 'gross gym joke' to actual comedy. The audience can picture it completely. Good absurdist specificity.

jokes.text: After Trey describes the barf-ball disaster, Abbi says 'It's actually not a dust brush job' — correcting the tool categorization for a barf-covered bouncing exercise ball
jokes.setup: Trey tells Abbi to grab her 'dust brush or whatever' to deal with the barf ball
jokes.punchline: Abbi's pedantic professional correction — she knows her cleaning tools and this correction is both completely accurate and hilariously insufficient as a response to the described chaos
jokes.explanation: Abbi's professionalism about the wrong thing — her first instinct is taxonomic accuracy about cleaning supplies, not horror at the barf. Very character-specific and economical. Works as a window into Abbi's earnest try-hard personality.

jokes.text: The coworker's gossip opener: 'Oh my God, Abbi, we have to dish. I heard one of the cleaners got a nose ring. What is it, 2004?'
jokes.setup: The coworker approaches Abbi excitedly to gossip... to Abbi herself, who has the nose ring
jokes.punchline: She unknowingly repeats the exact '2004' joke Abbi already heard from Ilana this morning — and is gossiping to the subject of the gossip. Abbi's awkward 'Uh... rea... cool' sells it.
jokes.explanation: Strong structural joke — the callback to '2004' adds a layer (the joke is now at Abbi's expense), and the situational irony of gossiping to the subject herself is well-executed. The payoff is Abbi's fumbled non-response. The rewatch bonus applies because you notice the deliberate echo of the earlier joke.

jokes.text: Abbi says 'I don't think he really wants me there. I feel like it was like, a pity invite' about Trey's party
jokes.setup: Coworker tells Abbi she HAS to come to Trey's party, that Trey talks about how funny she is all the time
jokes.punchline: Abbi's complete inability to accept that someone genuinely likes her is a character-consistent joke — her self-deprecation is so deep she interprets genuine warmth as pity
jokes.explanation: Competent character comedy that tracks with Abbi's insecurity arc throughout the series, but it's not a particularly surprising beat. The show sets this up only to pay it off ironically at the end, so it functions better as setup than standalone comedy.

jokes.text: 'I mean, I'm pretty sure I'm not funny...' / 'Oh my God, you are so weird. I love it.'
jokes.setup: Coworker says Trey thinks Abbi is hilarious. Abbi deflects.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's denial of being funny is itself funny, and the coworker's response ('you are so weird') accidentally confirms it without meaning to — she's calling Abbi weird when Abbi said she wasn't funny, subtly proving Abbi's point
jokes.explanation: There's a subtle secondary joke in 'you are so weird, I love it' as a response to 'I'm not funny' — weird ≠ funny. The coworker is enthusiastically missing the point. Functional character comedy.

jokes.text: Abbi's reaction: 'Uh... rea... cool.' — clearly flustered and not knowing how to respond
jokes.setup: Gemma has just trashed Ilana's nose ring directly to Abbi without recognizing her
jokes.punchline: Abbi's completely failed attempt to form a response — the awkward non-word 'rea' is the joke
jokes.explanation: A small but real laugh — Abbi's social paralysis is relatable and the fractured syllable 'rea' is a specific, honest acting choice. Functional cringe comedy, not a marquee moment.

jokes.text: Ilana bursts into an office conference room mid-meeting to announce she needs to leave because her best friend is going to a hot party
jokes.setup: Serious-looking office meeting in progress; Ilana's colleague is delivering a motivational speech about self-worth
jokes.punchline: Ilana interrupts with 'I do have a horrible emergency... my best friend Abbi is going to a hot party tonight and now I gotta leave right now' — FOMO as a legitimate workplace emergency
jokes.explanation: Pure Ilana — the complete equivalence in her mind between a real emergency and FOMO. The 'horrible emergency' setup makes the actual reason land hard. Strong character integration; only Ilana would treat this as legitimate grounds to leave a business meeting. The 'Flight of the Bumblebees' musical sting confirms she's running out immediately.

jokes.text: Boss grants Ilana leave 'only because you closed that keratin pube straightening deal'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks to leave for FOMO; boss weighs whether she's earned it
jokes.punchline: 'The keratin pube straightening deal' — an absurdly specific and disgusting professional accomplishment used as legitimate job currency
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist joke. 'Keratin pube straightening' is simultaneously a real product that could exist (keratin is a hair treatment) and completely unhinged as a corporate deal. The boss saying it with complete professional seriousness elevates it. Maximum funny per word. One of the episode's sharper lines.

jokes.text: Ilana tells her coworker Nicole: 'I left a hardboiled egg in our cube. You can have it if you can find it.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is rushing out and says a quick goodbye to Nicole
jokes.punchline: The hardboiled egg hidden somewhere in their shared workspace — both as a casual snack gift and as an inexplicable egg-hiding behavior that raises more questions than it answers
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliant tiny joke. 'You can have it if you can find it' is doing enormous comedic work: it implies Ilana routinely hides food in her workspace, that this is somehow an Easter-egg-style scavenger hunt she's left as a parting gift, and that Nicole will now spend her afternoon searching for a hardboiled egg. Maximum economy — nine words that create an entire absurd world. The specificity of 'hardboiled egg' (not a snack bar, not a banana) is perfect. Strong contender for best joke in the episode.

jokes.text: Abbi asks 'Why does Trey live next to Port Authority?' while they walk to the party
jokes.setup: They're heading to Trey's party; the implication is they're in a gross/inconvenient part of NYC
jokes.punchline: Port Authority Bus Terminal is widely considered one of the most unpleasant spots in Manhattan — the question is funny because the answer is obvious (he's cheap/unfortunate) but Abbi asks it with genuine curiosity
jokes.explanation: A mild NYC-specific observational joke. Works for a local audience but requires knowledge that Port Authority is a punchline in itself. Not particularly crafted, just a quick location gag.

jokes.text: Abbi says she'll use the party to 'finally spill the beans' to Trey about wanting to be an instructor/trainer; Ilana responds 'Hell yeah, dude, carpe damn'
jokes.setup: Abbi is nervous about telling her boss her career ambitions
jokes.punchline: 'Carpe damn' — Ilana's garbled version of 'carpe diem,' which is either intentional wordplay or characteristically Ilana-esque linguistic chaos
jokes.explanation: 'Carpe damn' is the kind of mangled inspirational phrase that's become a Broad City signature — it sounds like a mistake but is clearly deliberate, and it works because Ilana's support is genuine even if her Latin is broken. Quotable.

jokes.text: Trey hosts a party that turns out to be a tiny, lame gathering — the visual of Abbi and Ilana walking in and immediately needing to leave
jokes.setup: Ilana speculates they're 'extremely early'; Trey says 'Nope, you're the last to arrive'
jokes.punchline: The full party is visible — a sad, small gathering of fitness bros watching DVDs — and both Abbi and Ilana's immediate responses ('We have to go' / 'I'm embarrassed to be here') confirm the visual horror
jokes.explanation: The deadpan reveal structure is classic situational comedy — the build-up of anticipation for the party vs. the sad reality. 'You're the last to arrive' is the perfect gut-punch line. Works as a visual gag even from the transcript.

jokes.text: Ilana says Abbi is being 'intolerant' for wanting to leave; Abbi gasps
jokes.setup: Ilana tells Abbi they can't leave the lame party
jokes.punchline: Using 'intolerant' — a word with heavy social justice connotations in their world — to mean 'unwilling to tolerate a bad party.' Abbi gasps as if genuinely accused of bigotry.
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay that plays on Ilana's politically charged vocabulary being redirected toward trivial concerns. It's a quick joke that says something true about these characters' social world. Economy is strong.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Ilana, you're being... intolerant.' — then Trey arrives
jokes.setup: Ilana has been rude about the lame party and its guests
jokes.punchline: Abbi frames Ilana's snobbery about party quality as 'intolerance' — a word loaded with social justice meaning being repurposed for trivial party standards
jokes.explanation: Clever word repurposing — 'intolerant' in 2014 carries specific social weight that Abbi is using to describe Ilana's party standards. Self-aware cultural commentary packaged as a quick gag.

jokes.text: Trey offers party snacks: 'Clif bars for the guys, Lunas for the girls'
jokes.setup: Trey invites Abbi to 'help herself to snacks'
jokes.punchline: His gendered snack segregation is both a real fitness industry thing and a perfect capsule of Trey's oblivious bro worldview — offered with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: Tight, efficient character joke. Trey isn't being malicious — he genuinely thinks this is hospitality. The specificity (Luna bars are actually marketed toward women) grounds the absurdity. Works as a quick Trey characterization moment.

jokes.text: The coworker/Gemma whispers 'What a freak' approvingly about Abbi after the snack selection
jokes.setup: Abbi does or says something innocuous
jokes.punchline: Gemma's enthusiastic 'What a freak' as a compliment — she is constantly interpreting Abbi as far more fascinating and eccentric than Abbi actually is
jokes.explanation: A small running character gag with Gemma seeing Abbi as endlessly quirky and cool. Functional but not deeply crafted — the joke is consistent but thin.

jokes.text: Abbi says unprompted: 'I would definitely rather turn into a little old man than a little old woman, but I'm not going to be able to help it'
jokes.setup: Gemma says they should 'dish' about 'girl stuff'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's response to 'girl stuff' is an existential reflection on the aesthetics of aging — completely sincere, utterly random, and somehow exactly the right move to make Gemma love her even more
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely great Abbi joke. The setup is 'let's talk about girl stuff' — the expected response is gossip or fashion talk. Abbi instead delivers a completely sincere, oddly philosophical observation about gender and aging that she has clearly thought about. The 'but I'm not going to be able to help it' makes it even funnier because she's resigned. Only Abbi. Strong originality and character integration.

jokes.text: Gemma asks 'Do you ever straighten your hair, though?' and Abbi clarifies 'Wait, do you mean my head hair?'
jokes.setup: Hair straightening is a normal conversation topic between women
jokes.punchline: Abbi's clarification implies she straightens some other kind of hair — the laugh is in the implication and Gemma's delighted 'Oh!' response confirming she got the reference
jokes.explanation: Economy is near-perfect — four words ('do you mean my head hair?') do all the work. The implication is clear without being explicit. Quick, sharp, and plays on the casual intimacy of the Broad City universe where body humor is normal. The 'Oh!' reaction from Gemma makes it land.

jokes.text: The Apprentice 'All-Stars' / 'Omarosa' watching DVDs reveal — the party activity is watching reality TV reruns
jokes.setup: Trey says 'pick a hand' as if something fun is coming
jokes.punchline: The 'prize' for choosing wrong is The Apprentice All-Stars on DVD; the prize for choosing right is implied to be even worse, which the show mercifully omits
jokes.explanation: A nice gag — the party's entertainment is Apprentice DVDs. The 'pick a hand' structure implies a game; the non-prize of reality TV DVDs lands the lame-party concept. 'Omarosa' as a one-word enthusiastic response from a guest deepens the joke.

jokes.text: Trey's 'Pick a hand' game where no matter which hand you pick, you watch 'The Apprentice All-Stars'
jokes.setup: Trey does a classic 'pick a hand' game implying one option is exciting
jokes.punchline: Both hands lead to 'The Apprentice All-Stars' — it's not a real choice, it was always going to be this, Trey is deeply uncool
jokes.explanation: A classic misdirection structure used to confirm Trey's lameness — his party idea of fun is a DVD of a cancelled reality show. The 'Oh yeah, Omarosa' callback from Abbi sells that she's also mildly intrigued before reality sets back in. Competent situational comedy.

jokes.text: Ilana has texted 'where party' to EVERYONE in her phone the moment the party disappointed her
jokes.setup: Ilana tells Abbi the night is 'ruined'
jokes.punchline: 'The moment we got to the party, I texted 'where party' to everybody in my phone' — she did this immediately and indiscriminately, to everyone, including presumably parents and coworkers
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the scope — not 'I texted some friends' but 'everybody in my phone.' And the exact phrasing 'where party' (not 'where's the party') is perfectly Ilana — ungrammatical, chaotic, efficient. Classic Ilana move; strong character integration.

jokes.text: Abbi tells Trey she has a tapeworm to get out of the party
jokes.setup: Abbi needs an excuse to leave Trey's party — this is her boss who likes her
jokes.punchline: She claims to have a tapeworm — an illness choice that is both dramatic enough to justify leaving AND obscure/gross enough to be completely unbelievable
jokes.explanation: The tapeworm lie is great because it's specific enough to seem considered but absurd enough to be obviously false. Abbi's choice of illness reveals her panic — she reaches for something dramatic and medical-sounding. This joke rewards rewatch because we know how it pays off later when Trey sees her on Instagram.

jokes.text: Trey responds to the tapeworm news: 'Abbi, that's really dangerous. I'm pretty sure tapeworms suck nutrients out of your body. That must be why you ate four and a half luna bars.'
jokes.setup: Abbi makes up a tapeworm diagnosis
jokes.punchline: Trey believes her AND helpfully provides parasitology facts AND connects it to the luna bar consumption — he is fully committed to worrying about his employee's fictional tapeworm
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed joke. Trey's concern is genuine — he's a fitness guy who actually knows about nutrition and parasites. The callback to the luna bars ('that must be why you ate four and a half') is a perfect structural callback that makes the lie even worse because now Abbi has inadvertently created retroactive 'evidence' for her tapeworm. The specificity of 'four and a half luna bars' is the cherry on top. Very strong.

jokes.text: Abbi escalates the tapeworm lie: 'I feel like it's moving and like, now it's in my bu... uh... It's in my butt... now.'
jokes.setup: Trey is now genuinely concerned; Abbi has committed to the tapeworm lie and is making it worse
jokes.punchline: The lie escalates to real-time tapeworm location updates culminating in 'it's in my butt... now' — the pause and hesitation before 'butt' suggests she knows she's gone too far even as she says it
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the tapeworm location is a great cringe beat. The hesitation ('bu... uh...') makes it land harder than if she said it cleanly — it suggests Abbi knows she's digging the hole deeper. Trey's horrified 'Hmmm!' reaction completes the moment.

jokes.text: Trey announces to the whole party: 'Guys, pause it. Abbi had to go because she's got a tapeworm in her butt.'
jokes.setup: Abbi just wants to sneak out; Trey turns it into a group announcement
jokes.punchline: Trey broadcasts the tapeworm-in-butt information to everyone at the party — the social horror is maximized. The guests' laughter ('She is such a riot') suggests they don't believe her either.
jokes.explanation: Trey announcing 'tapeworm in her butt' to the party is simultaneously the natural consequence of Abbi's lie escalating AND foreshadows the party guests thinking she's funny (which pays off at the end). The guests laughing and calling her a riot ('Gemma, no bazinga') adds another layer — they think she's joking, which she sort of is, but not intentionally. Good structure.

jokes.text: Gemma laughs and says 'She is such a riot' about Abbi's tapeworm announcement; Trey responds 'Gemma, no... no bazinga'
jokes.setup: Trey wants people to understand this is a genuine medical emergency
jokes.punchline: 'No bazinga' — Trey's absolute belief in the tapeworm, and his repurposing of 'bazinga' as a word for 'just kidding' when correcting someone who IS treating the situation as a joke
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'bazinga' is excellent. Trey introduced it as a prank/joking word; now he uses 'no bazinga' to signal THIS IS NOT A JOKE, I ACTUALLY BELIEVE MY EMPLOYEE HAS A TAPEWORM. The irony is maximized — the word established as 'this is a joke' is now being used to insist something is real. Great structural callback.

jokes.text: Ilana to Jaime's boyfriend: 'I've been social media stalking you since Jaime told me you were his... whatever'
jokes.setup: Meeting Jaime's new boyfriend at the party
jokes.punchline: 'His... whatever' — Ilana's refusal to assign relationship labels is both ideologically consistent and slightly offensive when said to the person's face
jokes.explanation: Consistent Ilana characterization — her anti-labeling politics applied in a somewhat awkward social situation. Functional character comedy, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Ilana's extended speech about not fetishizing gay friends: 'Not like in a 'I can't wait to officiate your gay wedding' way, 'cause straight people always say that and it's like, gross. It's just fetishizing their gay friends.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to express genuine warmth to Jaime's boyfriend
jokes.punchline: The overly elaborate disclaimer about NOT being that kind of ally becomes its own form of the thing she's disclaiming — a performative display of woke awareness that's still about her
jokes.explanation: Ilana's self-aware allyship becoming its own performance is a genuinely smart joke about the character and about progressive social dynamics. The 'maybe you'll just fuck for a hot sec and move on and that is hot too' is a great secondary punchline — she can't help making it about sex. The speech is a bit long (economy dips) but pays off well.

jokes.text: After the whole speech, the boyfriend simply says 'You're Ilana, right?' and Ilana says 'How did you know?'
jokes.setup: Ilana delivers a lengthy speech about allyship and gay relationships
jokes.punchline: 'You're Ilana, right?' — her reputation precedes her. Jaime has apparently described her so specifically that this speech immediately confirms her identity. Ilana's 'How did you know?' is perfect self-unawareness.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured. The setup is the long speech; the punchline is two words that do everything. 'You're Ilana, right?' tells us Jaime has talked about her, that she is exactly as described, and that her 'uniqueness' is so consistent it's recognizable. 'How did you know?' is the character's perfect blind spot — she doesn't see that she just defined herself perfectly. Economy is near-perfect: the scene sets up the speech and the payoff is six words. This is one of the episode's top jokes.

jokes.text: Instagram filter debate: 'I'm gonna use valencia, is that okay you think?' / 'Everybody uses the valencia' / 'What are you going to use?' / 'The Kelvin' / 'Using the kelv... no, that's silly, for goodness!' / 'For goodness.'
jokes.setup: Taking a photo for Instagram at the party
jokes.punchline: The absurdly earnest debate over Instagram filters as a serious social matter, culminating in 'for goodness' as a standalone exclamation — the mundane becomes the profound
jokes.explanation: Captures the show's vibe of making mundane digital-age rituals into comedy, but it's somewhat dated (Instagram filter discourse was more relevant in 2014). 'For goodness' as a recurring expression is mildly amusing. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Ilana's party-rating system: 'This party is a seven. We could be missing out on a 10.' Abbi: 'Yeah, but we leave and we end up at a six?'
jokes.setup: Ilana wants to leave yet another party
jokes.punchline: They've developed a numerical party-rating system with real stakes — and Abbi demonstrates she's internalized it despite being ostensibly the reasonable one
jokes.explanation: The party-rating system is a good absurdist framework that also reveals how Abbi has been gradually infected by Ilana's FOMO logic. Abbi arguing the downside risk ('end up at a six') shows she's already bought in. Good character development disguised as comedy.

jokes.text: Ilana's response to ending up at a six: 'I don't know, we fuck ourselves, who cares?'
jokes.setup: Abbi raises the legitimate concern of ending up at a worse party
jokes.punchline: Ilana's existentialist shrug — 'we kill ourselves, who cares' as a response to a bad party rating — is both absurdly nihilistic and characteristically Ilana's way of ending a logical impasse
jokes.explanation: The casual nihilism landing as the punchline to a party-rating debate is perfectly absurd. The economy is strong — it's a one-liner that ends the argument. The immediate pivot to 'So if we did have to kill ourselves how would we do it?' makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana seriously discuss suicide methods: 'I'd shoot you in the face and shoot myself in the heart.' 'Why would you shoot me in the face?' 'It's nice, you'd die instantly.' 'Well, then shoot me in the head, not in the face.'
jokes.setup: Following Ilana's 'we kill ourselves, who cares' — they take the hypothetical seriously
jokes.punchline: The negotiation over execution method as a tender act of friendship — Ilana's 'it's nice, you'd die instantly' and Abbi's logistical counter-proposal about head vs. face. The love language here is grotesque.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The escalation from party ratings to genuine murder-suicide method debate is perfectly Broad City — the dark subject is treated as pure logistics, and the tenderness underneath it ('it's nice, you'd die instantly') makes it paradoxically sweet. 'Shoot me in the head, not in the face' as Abbi's negotiating position is both gruesome and completely reasonable as a preference. High originality, strong character integration (only these two characters could have this exchange), and genuine escalation craft. Justifies 8+ with the caliber of its specific construction.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Why would you shoot me in the face?' / Ilana: 'It's nice, you'd die instantly.' / Abbi: 'Well, then shoot me in the head, not in the face.'
jokes.setup: Ilana proposed shooting Abbi in the face as a mercy kill
jokes.punchline: Abbi's complaint is not about being shot — it's about the specific facial damage. The face-versus-head distinction reveals Abbi's body-image concerns even in a fantasy death scenario.
jokes.explanation: Justifying the 8.5: This is the best joke in the episode. The genius is that Abbi's objection to being shot in the face versus the head is CHARACTER-SPECIFIC — Abbi has body image issues — and it's also absurdist escalation at its finest, and it's also a perfect piece of economy. She is accepting her murder but requesting the cosmetically preferable execution method. It would ONLY be funnier if delivered by someone with Abbi's established insecurities. Approaches the 8.0 anchor with authority and exceeds it. Genuinely original construction.

jokes.text: Lincoln claims to have 'just done the worm' at the party; when challenged, he demonstrates a version where he 'barely moves'
jokes.setup: 'You guys missed it, I just did the worm.' / 'You can do the worm?' / 'Yeah.'
jokes.punchline: 'You barely move' — Lincoln's worm is presumably a terrible, minimal worm that looks nothing like the dance move, yet he has full confidence in it
jokes.explanation: A physical comedy beat that's better in the actual episode than on the page. 'You barely move' is the punchline from the transcript but the real comedy is presumably visual — Lincoln doing a very sad worm. Competent but dependent on performance.

jokes.text: Lincoln does 'pec jumps' (makes his pectoral muscles bounce individually) and Ilana gasps and says 'Do that again'
jokes.setup: Following the worm revelation, Lincoln escalates to another physical feat
jokes.punchline: The pec jump is presumably impressive/absurd, and Ilana's completely sincere 'Do that again' is both objectifying and reverent — she's genuinely delighted
jokes.explanation: Another visual gag that plays better on screen. Ilana's physical enthusiasm about Lincoln's body is a consistent character beat. Functional.

jokes.text: Ilana commands Lincoln: 'Can you worm us there?' — and he does the worm across the party toward the snacks
jokes.setup: They want to get food
jokes.punchline: Lincoln uses the worm as locomotion to escort them to the charcuterie table — a completely unnecessary use of a dance move
jokes.explanation: The worm as transportation is absurdist and physical. Good energy, not a standout gag but fun.

jokes.text: Ilana describes the charcuterie board as having 'dog dick, ghost poop, grapes, and cornichons'
jokes.setup: Looking at a charcuterie board at the new party
jokes.punchline: Ilana's food naming system — 'dog dick' and 'ghost poop' for what are presumably sausages and cheese — delivered completely matter-of-factly alongside 'grapes and cornichons'
jokes.explanation: Ilana's naming conventions for charcuterie are genuinely funny — 'dog dick' and 'ghost poop' are specific and visual enough to land while being surprising. The list structure (gross, gross, normal, normal) creates a nice rhythm. High quotability. Strongly Ilana.

jokes.text: Ilana holds up a cornichon next to her face: 'Don't I look like a giant?' / Abbi: 'That is so weird. I was just googling micropenises this morning and they look exactly like that. All lumpy and—'
jokes.setup: Ilana does a gag with cornichons to look like a giant
jokes.punchline: Abbi's completely unrelated morning Google search about micropenises — delivered conversationally as if it's totally normal to google micropenises before work — and her anatomical comparison
jokes.explanation: Abbi's matter-of-fact 'I was just googling micropenises this morning' is a great Abbi joke — she says it like it's a totally normal thing to have been doing, and the specificity of 'this morning' makes it funnier. The visual comparison to cornichons is absurdist and works well.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I was just googling micropenises this morning and they look exactly like that. All lumpy and (bleep).'
jokes.setup: Ilana is comparing the cornichon's appearance to something
jokes.punchline: The googling of micropenises, the 'this morning' specificity (just a casual research project), and the anatomical precision of 'all lumpy and (bleep)' — a morning micropenis google session is completely on-brand
jokes.explanation: The character integration here is excellent — it would be impossible to attribute this line to any other character on television. The specificity of 'this morning' makes it a character confession, not just a raunchy joke.

jokes.text: 'Come on, don't ruin cornichons for me. You saw I was enjoying this.' / 'Okay, don't ruin micropenises for me.' / 'Touché.'
jokes.setup: Abbi complains that Ilana is ruining cornichons with the micropenis comparison
jokes.punchline: Ilana's symmetrical counter that Abbi is ruining micropenises for HER, followed by Abbi's genuine 'Touché' — they've reached philosophical détente
jokes.explanation: This is a near-perfect quick joke. 'Don't ruin micropenises for me' lands because: (1) it implies Ilana has a warm relationship with micropenises, (2) it's the structurally symmetrical response to Abbi's complaint, and (3) 'Touché' as Abbi's genuine concession is perfect. Maximum economy. The whole exchange is four lines that tell you everything about their friendship. Justifies 8+ by its structural perfection and quotability.

jokes.text: 'The Narnia of partias is still out there and we're going to find it.' / 'Did you just say Narnia of partias?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is rallying Abbi to keep party-hopping
jokes.punchline: 'Narnia of partias' — a portmanteau of Narnia (magical promised land) and 'partias' (plural of party in invented Ilana-speak), which Abbi correctly identifies as a made-up word while Ilana says it with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: 'Narnia of partias' is excellent Ilana language — it works as a concept (the perfect legendary party) and as a word crime. The rewatch bonus applies because this sets up the Val bar being literally revealed as the Narnia. Strong quotability and character integration; only Ilana would coin this term.

jokes.text: The extended 'Rent' discourse: 'Do you guys know that play 'Rent'? I don't understand how they thought that they just didn't need to pay rent, like, at all.' / 'I know, it's like, it sucks, but everybody has to pay rent.'
jokes.setup: Party conversation takes an unexpected turn
jokes.punchline: Taking the title of 'Rent' completely literally — as if the bohemians were just freeloaders — is a deliberately dumb reading of the musical that says something true about late-capitalist NYC reality
jokes.explanation: The Rent bit works as both a joke about the musical and as a zeitgeist joke for broke NYC millennials — the show's actual audience probably relates more to 'everybody has to pay rent' than to bohemian romanticization. Slightly undercut by being in the background of a chaotic party scene.

jokes.text: 'I could totally be on Broadway. I'd be like... 500, 25...' (phone vibrates, interrupting)
jokes.setup: Following the Rent discussion, Ilana starts to demonstrate her Broadway potential
jokes.punchline: The interruption comes before she can even complete the lyric — we'll never know if Ilana can actually sing '525,600 minutes' — but the confidence of her lead-up is the joke
jokes.explanation: A nice subverted setup — we expect a terrible singing moment and it's cut off. The confidence is the joke. Functional but reliant on the interruption structure.

jokes.text: Trey calling to tell Abbi he saw her Instagram at a party with 'a drink on your head' — while she claimed to be in the hospital with a tapeworm
jokes.setup: Abbi's tapeworm lie is fully established; she's been drink-on-head partying
jokes.punchline: She's been caught via Instagram — the specific detail of 'a drink on your head' makes it worse because it's clearly a party photo, not a medical emergency
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural payoff — the episode plants both the tapeworm lie and the Instagram photo taking, and here they collide. 'A drink on your head' is the specific detail that makes it undeniable. Good earned comedy from threads planted earlier.

jokes.text: Abbi's cover story: 'It was a throwback Thursday pic that I put up.' / 'But it's Friday.' / 'I'm just stupid.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is caught on Instagram while claiming to be hospitalized
jokes.punchline: 'I'm just stupid' — when the TBT defense collapses immediately, Abbi's fallback is self-deprecation. It's somehow both a terrible cover story AND her most honest moment of the episode.
jokes.explanation: Three lines, two jokes: the TBT defense is itself a joke (it's obviously not true), and 'I'm just stupid' as the final fallback is somehow both completely wrong (she's panicking and creative, not stupid) and perfectly right as self-assessment of the situation she's created. Economy is excellent. Quintessential Abbi.

jokes.text: Lincoln improvises: 'Hello, hello, this is Dr. Rotreiller, MD.' / Trey: 'Are you a specialist?' / Lincoln: 'I'm more of a general jack-of-all-trades style doctor.'
jokes.setup: Abbi offers to put her 'doctor' on the phone to validate the tapeworm story
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's fake doctor name is 'Rotreiller' (Rottweiler), and his specialty is 'general jack-of-all-trades style doctor' — not a reassuring medical credential
jokes.explanation: 'Jack-of-all-trades style doctor' is an inspired phrase — vague enough to exist, specific enough to be hilarious. The 'style doctor' construction is particularly good. 'Rotreiller' is a fun name.

jokes.text: Dr. Rotreiller's tapeworm update: 'It's still in her anus. It is crowned and... and breeched.'
jokes.setup: Trey asks about tapeworm status, Ilana is ad-libbing medical information
jokes.punchline: 'Crowned and breeched' — childbirth terminology applied to tapeworm extraction, delivered with confused medical gravity. She's mixing up parasite removal with labor and delivery.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely excellent absurdist joke. 'Crowned and breeched' is obstetric terminology for a baby's presentation during labor — applying it to a tapeworm exiting someone's anus is both medically specific (showing Ilana actually knows these words) and perfectly wrong. The confusion of anal parasite removal with childbirth is inspired. High originality — this exact joke has not existed before. Justifies 8+ by the unexpected specificity and the image it creates.

jokes.text: Trey: 'Sorry, no offense, are you a nurse or are you a real doctor?' / Lincoln: 'I, uh... they're calling me. I gotta go get the worm... is... taken out.'
jokes.setup: Trey is reasonably suspicious of Dr. Rotreiller's credentials
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's verbal collapse — 'gotta go get the worm... is... taken out' — is a perfectly performed unraveling of someone improvising and running out of ideas
jokes.explanation: 'Get the worm... is... taken out' is a grammatical collapse in real time — Lincoln trying to say 'get the worm taken out' but fragmenting mid-sentence as his lie deteriorates. Performance-dependent but the writing sets it up well.

jokes.text: After Ilana hangs up, Abbi says 'I'm sorry. That guy did not sound qualified.'
jokes.setup: Ilana just impersonated a doctor on the phone to Abbi's boss
jokes.punchline: Abbi's flat understatement — 'that guy did not sound qualified' — as if she's a neutral observer commenting on a real doctor rather than the person who just put Ilana up to it
jokes.explanation: Abbi's deadpan disavowal is very funny because she's been complicit the whole time. 'That guy' as if she has no idea who it was. The economy is strong. Nice character beat.

jokes.text: Lincoln's 'trust fall' into a stranger's arms at a party — 'Ready to fall.' (Guests) 'Fall away.' 'Falling.'
jokes.setup: The scene opens on Lincoln at a different party
jokes.punchline: Lincoln has apparently integrated trust falls into his social behavior so thoroughly that the party guests know the call-and-response — 'Fall away' as a practiced reply suggests this has happened before
jokes.explanation: The trust fall as Lincoln's social ritual is a good character beat. The fact that guests know to respond 'Fall away' is funnier than the fall itself — it implies this is a known Lincoln move. Visual gag that's better performed.

jokes.text: After the trust fall, Lincoln clarifies: 'I'm a dentist, not a doctor. I don't know how to deal with high-pressure situations.'
jokes.setup: The trust fall nearly goes wrong; someone reacts; Lincoln explains himself
jokes.punchline: A dentist not knowing how to handle high-pressure situations is both a self-aware profession joke AND completely illogical — dentists deal with extreme anxiety all day. Lincoln's self-assessment is endearingly wrong.
jokes.explanation: A great Lincoln character joke — his self-deprecating 'I'm a dentist, not a doctor' is a callback to the Star Trek 'I'm a doctor, not a...' format but applied to Lincoln's specific profession. The punchline is that dentists DO deal with high-pressure situations (their patients are terrified of them), which Lincoln seems unaware of. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Lincoln does a 'black Irish goodbye' — apparently just vanishes from a conversation without warning, and this is a known behavior
jokes.setup: Someone comments on Lincoln's sudden departure; a guest explains
jokes.punchline: 'It's a black Irish goodbye. He always does it.' — a hybrid of the 'Irish goodbye' (leaving a party without saying goodbye) with 'black' as a racial identifier, implying this is Lincoln's signature move. The racial specificity subverts the standard expression.
jokes.explanation: A clever subversion of the 'Irish goodbye' idiom — the 'black Irish goodbye' as Lincoln's specific version is a good wordplay joke that also characterizes Lincoln as someone who disappears when things get intense (as established by his trust fall anxiety). High quotability.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'All right, I agree.' — agreeing with the decision to go to the rooftop party after initially resisting
jokes.setup: Abbi said she wanted to go home; Ilana suggests one more party
jokes.punchline: Abbi folds completely — 'All right, I agree' with 'Yessa!' as the irrationally enthusiastic acceptance — her earlier resolve entirely gone
jokes.explanation: A small character-comedy beat. Abbi's complete capitulation is funny but not a standout. Functional episode tissue.

jokes.text: They arrive at the rooftop party. Ilana: 'Whoo, this party is a— 10, I knew it!' then: 'Yes, whose party is this, dude? Who gives a fat wet (bleep)?'
jokes.setup: They've been chasing the perfect party all night
jokes.punchline: The rooftop IS a 10 — but the price is complete anonymity, they have no idea whose party it is, and Ilana's answer to this is 'who gives a fat wet (bleep)?' — the FOMO logic fully inverted
jokes.explanation: Good payoff on the rating system — they finally found the 10. The 'fat wet (bleep)' construction is a nice intensifier on the basic 'who cares.' The point is made: the Narnia of partias doesn't care who you are.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'God, I love when straight dudes make out.'
jokes.setup: Two straight guys apparently kiss at the rooftop party
jokes.punchline: Ilana's complete reversal of the expected dynamic — typically it's straight men fetishizing women kissing. Ilana turning this around with genuine delight is both a character moment and a social commentary joke.
jokes.explanation: Eight words with significant comedic and ideological content. Perfectly Ilana — her sexuality is omnivorous and her commentary on gender double standards is delivered as pure enjoyment rather than lecture. Extreme economy: zero wasted words. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: 'You never know if you never try, you never try if you never know, you know?'
jokes.setup: Watching the straight guys make out; someone offers wisdom
jokes.punchline: The circular, self-canceling aphorism — 'you never know if you never try, you never try if you never know' is a tautology that means nothing — delivered as if it's profound insight
jokes.explanation: A nicely constructed piece of nonsense wisdom — the circular logic self-destructs on examination but sounds confident. The final 'you know?' invites agreement on a statement that makes no sense. Solid quick joke.

jokes.text: 'You never know if you never try, you never try if you never know, you know?'
jokes.setup: Said by someone presumably after the straight-guys-making-out moment
jokes.punchline: A circular pseudo-philosophy — 'you never know if you never try, you never try if you never know' — that is logically incoherent but sounds profound when drunk
jokes.explanation: The chiasmus is intentionally circular — the second half reverses the first creating a tautology that means nothing. Good party-wisdom absurdism.

jokes.text: Bouncer scene: Ilana tries to get them into a venue, fails, then: 'They're not gonna let us in, then I don't wanna go in there. 'Cause you know why? We deserve better than that.'
jokes.setup: They've been rejected from a venue
jokes.punchline: Ilana's immediate pivot from wanting in to claiming they're too good for it — ego protection as philosophy — 'We deserve better' for a random club they just tried to enter
jokes.explanation: Classic rejection rationalization. Character-specific because Ilana genuinely believes this. The speed of the reversal is the joke.

jokes.text: Val's introduction: 'I'm Val about town, town about Val.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has apparently blacked out and become 'Val' — a completely different personality — at a dive bar
jokes.punchline: 'Town about Val' as a phrase — it doesn't quite make sense but Val delivers it with complete jazz-age confidence, which is itself the joke about this alternate persona
jokes.explanation: The introduction of Val — Abbi's blackout alter ego — is a magnificent piece of character comedy. 'I'm Val about town, town about Val' is deliberately nonsensical in a specific way: it's a reversal of 'man about town' that breaks the phrase while maintaining the swagger. The delivery implies Val has said this many times. The reveal that Abbi has a full blackout persona is the episode's central comedic invention. High quotability, high originality, strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: The entire bar greets Val by name: '(Man) Hey, it's Val! (All) Val!'
jokes.setup: 'Who is Val?' — Ilana's confused question about the person Abbi is becoming
jokes.punchline: The bar erupts in recognition — Val is KNOWN here, she's a regular, she has people. Abbi has no idea this version of herself exists and has a whole social life.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best reveal moment. The visual of an entire bar cheering for 'Val' — a person Abbi doesn't know she is — is both a perfect comedy beat and a rich character insight: somewhere inside anxious, self-doubting Abbi is a charismatic jazz-age queen. The setup through Ilana's 'who is Val?' question makes the answer land like a freight train. Justifies 8+ easily: it's the episode's conceptual centerpiece delivered with maximum impact.

jokes.text: Val/Abbi performs a full swing song: '♪ Forget your troubles, come on get happy ♪' — the whole bar is her audience
jokes.setup: Abbi is normally shy and self-doubting
jokes.punchline: Val/Abbi is performing an entire song confidently for a rapt bar audience — a total personality inversion
jokes.explanation: The extended Val performance is primarily visual/performance comedy. The character irony is rich but the humor lives in Jacobson's physicality. On paper it scores lower than in execution — the rewatch bonus accounts for the visual payoff of seeing reserved Abbi perform with total confidence.

jokes.text: Bartender reveals Val has been coming here for 'over a hundred years' / 'No, I'm just kidding, it's more like three years.'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks how long Val has been a regular
jokes.punchline: The bartender's deadpan 'over a hundred years' followed by instant correction — the absurd exaggeration gives way to the actually more surprising truth that Abbi has been doing this for three years
jokes.explanation: The fake-out is well-constructed — 'over a hundred years' is obviously wrong and funny; 'more like three years' is the real punchline because three years is still a long time for Abbi to have a secret identity. Ilana's 'Oh' lands as the double-take.

jokes.text: Ilana's realization: 'How did I not know that when Abbi gets blackout drunk she becomes Val? My FOMO's through the damn roof.'
jokes.setup: The Val revelation is sinking in for Ilana
jokes.punchline: FOMO now applies to Ilana's own best friend's secret identity — she's been missing out on Val this whole time. FOMO has turned inward and become existential.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's thematic payload delivered as a joke. Ilana — who has spent the entire episode causing FOMO anxiety in others — now has FOMO about her own best friend's hidden life. The callback to the episode's title concept lands perfectly here. Strong structure: the whole episode has been building to this inversion. Justifies 8+ as the joke that most earns its place in the episode architecture.

jokes.text: Val greets Ilana: 'Aren't you a hot diggity-diggity-dog and a scallywag to boot?'
jokes.setup: Val encounters Ilana for the first time as Val
jokes.punchline: Val's entire vernacular is swing-era slang used with complete sincerity — 'hot diggity-diggity-dog' and 'scallywag' as compliments from a 20-something woman in 2014
jokes.explanation: Val's dialect is the episode's greatest invention. 'Hot diggity-diggity-dog' is an escalation of the 1940s exclamation; 'scallywag to boot' is absolutely unhinged as a 2014 compliment. The fact that Ilana, who normally has the most outrageous language, is the one being linguistically outdone is a lovely reversal. High quotability.

jokes.text: Ilana's reaction to being called 'Baby Doll' by Val: 'Baby Doll? Of course I have been missing out on this for years.'
jokes.setup: Val calls Ilana 'Baby Doll'
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'of course' — she immediately recognizes that Val is exactly the kind of person she would have loved to have known about. The FOMO is now fully embodied.
jokes.explanation: A good reaction beat that closes the FOMO loop emotionally. Ilana's 'of course' is both funny (she immediately gets it) and a tiny bit sad (she's been missing this). Works as character comedy.

jokes.text: Val: 'You're always worried about missing out on life. You never bother to actually live.' / Ilana: 'Uh... doy.'
jokes.setup: Val delivers a profound observation about Ilana's FOMO anxiety
jokes.punchline: 'Uh... doy' — Ilana's dismissive/self-aware response to advice she both needs to hear and is too cool to be affected by. She's simultaneously acknowledging the wisdom and refusing to be moved by it.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's thematic statement delivered as a joke. Val's line is the show's actual message: Ilana's FOMO is its own form of missing out. But the genius is in Ilana's 'uh... doy' — she hears the wisdom, processes it in one beat, and dismisses it with 1990s valley-girl language. It's the funniest possible response because it confirms the point (she still won't change) while being completely character-perfect. Economy is extreme: three words capture an entire psychological reality. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Val kisses a bar patron: 'Johnny, you cad. I forgot you were the best kisser in town. Except for my main squeeze.' / Ilana: 'I'm your main squeeze?' / Val: 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Val has a whole social world in this bar; Ilana is an outsider to Val's life
jokes.punchline: When Val names her 'main squeeze,' it's Ilana — Val recognizes Ilana even from within the alternate persona — the friendship is Val-proof
jokes.explanation: Sweet and funny simultaneously. Abbi's subconscious (Val) still recognizes Ilana as her person — even in a blackout alter-ego state, the friendship holds. Good emotional underpinning to a comedic beat.

jokes.text: Val kisses a man: 'Johnny, you cad. I forgot you were the best kisser in town. Except for my main squeeze.'
jokes.setup: Val is socializing at the bar and kisses a man named Johnny
jokes.punchline: 'Johnny, you cad' — again, the swing-era vocabulary applied to what appears to be casual party making-out. Val has a whole romantic social world that Abbi is unaware of.
jokes.explanation: Another Val vocabulary joke that also deepens the Val mythology — she has a regular kissing partner named Johnny AND a 'main squeeze.' The 1940s moral framework applied to 2014 casual hook-up culture is consistently funny.

jokes.text: Val tells Ilana she's 'my favorite little jewy this side of St. Louie'
jokes.setup: Val calls Ilana her 'main squeeze'
jokes.punchline: 'Favorite little jewy this side of St. Louie' — the period slang for identifying someone as Jewish ('jewy'), delivered with complete affection in Val's framework, which is both startling and charming
jokes.explanation: A bold joke. 'My favorite little jewy' reclaims a potentially offensive diminutive within Val's 1940s idiom where 'jewy' was used casually and affectionately. The show can do this because Ilana herself is Jewish and the show's writing room is attuned to this. The reaction ('Whoa. Oh my God, dude') confirms the transgression is registered. It works because it's Val's framework, not present-day Abbi's.

jokes.text: Val is given a diamond and gasps with child-like delight
jokes.setup: Someone at the bar presents Val with what appears to be a diamond
jokes.punchline: Val's complete, sincere joy at receiving a diamond — presented as both a character beat (Val loves jewelry) and a visual gag (where did someone at a dive bar get a diamond?)
jokes.explanation: More of a visual beat than a joke — it deepens the Val character and the bar's magical realism. Functional scene element, not a strong standalone joke.

jokes.text: Ilana announces: 'Val bar is Narnia!'
jokes.setup: The whole Val bar experience
jokes.punchline: The payoff of 'Narnia of partias' — the legendary perfect party they were searching for was here all along, in a jazz bar, as Abbi's blackout alter ego. The FOMO quest ends at the source.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best structural joke. 'Val bar is Narnia' pays off: the Narnia metaphor established in joke #43, the Val reveal from joke #59, and the entire episode's FOMO theme. It's three words that resolve the episode's central comedic premise. The structure score is near-perfect because every element was planted and this payoff is both surprising and inevitable. Strong justification for 8+ — this is how you write a thematic callback.

jokes.text: Morning after: Ilana singing 'No mo' FOMO' while Abbi has absolutely no memory of Val
jokes.setup: Next morning, Abbi wakes up with no memory of Val
jokes.punchline: Ilana's triumphant 'No mo' FOMO' song vs. Abbi's complete blankness — Ilana has been cured of FOMO by experiencing Val; Abbi missed her own cure because she was the cure
jokes.explanation: A beautiful comedic/thematic landing for the episode. The irony is rich: Ilana, who caused all the FOMO, is now cured. Abbi, who just wanted a chill night, missed her own legendary performance. 'No mo' FOMO' as an improvised song captures Ilana's character perfectly. The structure is the best kind of sitcom payoff — everything is resolved but in a way that's simultaneously triumphant and tragic for Abbi. Justifies 8+ as the episode's emotional climax.

jokes.text: It's 6:45 AM. Abbi realizes she has work at 7. 'I guess I'm just gonna go to work right now then.'
jokes.setup: They're still out at dawn; Abbi thinks she'll go home and sleep before work
jokes.punchline: The immediate pivot from 'I'll go home and sleep' to 'it's 6:45' to 'I guess I'm just going to work right now' — delivered with completely resigned acceptance
jokes.explanation: Abbi's resignation is very funny — there's no panic, no drama, just 'well, I guess that's happening.' The economy of 'I guess I'm just gonna go to work right now then' as a response to realizing you've been up all night is perfectly deflated.

jokes.text: Ilana tells the cab driver 'Treat her right, sir' as Abbi gets in to go directly to work from a bar at 6:45 AM
jokes.setup: Abbi gets in a cab to go directly to work
jokes.punchline: Ilana's parting words — addressing the cab driver with formal concern for Abbi, as if Abbi's cab ride deserves the same gravity as seeing someone off on a journey — while also completely ignoring the fact that Abbi is about to go to work reeking of a bar
jokes.explanation: A small but good Ilana moment — the formal chivalry applied to a chaotic situation. The cab driver's nonplussed reaction (implied) completes it.

jokes.text: Abbi gets out of the cab, coughs, and someone says 'Classic Val'
jokes.setup: Abbi exits the cab at the gym, clearly hungover
jokes.punchline: 'Classic Val' — presumably said by someone who witnessed Val's exploits, delivered as if Val's aftermath is a well-known phenomenon. Val's legend extends even to this moment.
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the Val joke. 'Classic Val' as a reaction to Abbi arriving hungover at work continues the joke that Val is a recognizable archetype to everyone except Abbi herself. The economy is perfect — two words that contain the whole episode's joke. Good rewatch value.

jokes.text: Ilana grabs Abbi's nose ring area: 'Hold on, I think my nose ring is here.' Abbi: 'Don't touch it!'
jokes.setup: The nose ring from the episode's opening has been dormant
jokes.punchline: Ilana apparently still has her nose ring and goes to touch it on a whim, Abbi's immediate horror at potentially infecting/irritating a fresh piercing on another person is a late callback to the opening
jokes.explanation: A small callback that closes the nose ring thread from the opening. Works as a light bookend but isn't a strong standalone joke.

jokes.text: Maria explains: 'I always wanted to be a trainer, so I asked Trey at the party. We both were a little drunk, so I was not afraid. It was like asking a friend, you know, instead of a boss.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks how Maria became a trainer so quickly
jokes.punchline: Maria unknowingly describes the exact strategy Abbi had planned but failed to execute — she even uses the same framing ('like asking a friend instead of a boss') that Abbi used earlier. Maria did by accident what Abbi couldn't do on purpose.
jokes.explanation: This is the most precisely engineered callback in the episode. Maria's explanation mirrors Abbi's pre-party plan word for word — 'like asking a friend instead of a boss.' The audience planted this in Abbi's scene at [04:30] and here it lands as both comedy and tragedy: the thing Abbi planned to say, Maria actually said. The structure score is very high because this requires the writer to have planted the exact phrase earlier. Earned comedy at its finest. Second-highest structure score in the episode.
